from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of Triple objects into a single fluent sentence.
    """
    # Simple predicate‑to‑phrase mapping; fall back to a generic form.
    # More natural phrasing for key predicates
    verb_map = {
        "almaMater": "studied at",
        "birthDate": "was born on",
        "birthName": "was originally named",
        "birthPlace": "was born in",
        "dateOfRetirement": "retired on",
        "occupation": "worked as",
        "status": "is",                     # e.g., "is retired"
        "timeInSpace": "spent",             # will add "in space" later
        "mission": "took part in",
        "selectedByNasa": "was selected by NASA in",
        "deathDate": "died on",
        "nationality": "was a citizen of",
        "servedAsChiefOfTheAstronautOfficeIn": "served as Chief of the Astronaut Office in",
        "title": "held the title",
        "ribbonAward": "received the ribbon",
        "operator": "was operated by",
        "backupPilot": "had backup pilot",
        "commander": "was commanded by",
        "crewMembers": "had crew member",
        "representative": "was represented by",
        "alternativeName": "was also known as",
        "awards": "received",               # will add "awards" suffix if numeric
        "fossil": "has fossil",
        "senators": "has senator",
        "part": "is part of",
        "partsType": "has parts type",
        "higher": "is higher than",
        "isPartOf": "is part of",
        "bird": "has bird",
        "leader": "has leader",
        "affiliation": "is affiliated with",
        "competeIn": "competes in",
        "president": "has president",
        "award": "received award",
        "deathPlace": "died in",
        "gemstone": "has gemstone",
        "mascot": "has mascot",
        "cosparId": "has COSPAR ID",
        "utcOffset": "has UTC offset",
    }

    # Group triples by subject to build richer clauses
    if not triples:
        return ""
    grouped = {}
    for t in triples:
        grouped.setdefault(t.subject, []).append(t)

    sentences = []
    for subject, ts in grouped.items():
        # Define a preferred ordering for predicates to improve readability
        predicate_order = {
            "birthName": 0,
            "birthDate": 1,
            "birthPlace": 2,
            "nationality": 3,
            "almaMater": 4,
            "occupation": 5,
            "mission": 6,
            "selectedByNasa": 7,
            "status": 8,
            "timeInSpace": 9,
            "awards": 10,
            "operator": 11,
            "commander": 12,
            "backupPilot": 13,
            "crewMembers": 14,
            "title": 15,
            "ribbonAward": 16,
            "award": 17,
            "deathDate": 18,
            "deathPlace": 19,
            "representative": 20,
            "alternativeName": 21,
            "servedAsChiefOfTheAstronautOfficeIn": 22,
            # any other predicates will get a high default order
        }
        # Sort triples for this subject according to the defined order
        ts = sorted(ts, key=lambda tr: predicate_order.get(tr.predicate, 100))
        clause_parts = []
        for t in ts:
            # Determine verb phrase
            verb = verb_map.get(t.predicate, f"has {t.predicate}")
            # Clean object string
            obj = t.object.strip('"')
            # Special handling for some predicates
            if t.predicate == "timeInSpace":
                obj = f"{obj} minutes in space"
            elif t.predicate == "awards":
                # If the object looks numeric, add the word "awards"
                if obj.isdigit():
                    obj = f"{obj} awards"
            elif t.predicate == "status":
                # make status lowercase for smoother reading
                obj = obj.lower()
            clause_parts.append(f"{verb} {obj}")

        if len(clause_parts) == 1:
            sentence = f"{subject} {clause_parts[0]}"
        else:
            # join with commas and an 'and' before the last part
            sentence = f"{subject} " + ", ".join(clause_parts[:-1]) + " and " + clause_parts[-1]
        sentences.append(sentence)

    # Combine subject clauses; use semicolon for distinct subjects
    # Combine subject clauses into a single fluent sentence
    if len(sentences) == 1:
        final_sentence = sentences[0] + "."
    else:
        final_sentence = ", ".join(sentences[:-1]) + ", and " + sentences[-1] + "."
    # Capitalize first character
    return final_sentence[0].upper() + final_sentence[1:]

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END
